title: H. P. Lovecraft's Commonplace Book from 1919 to 1934
description: The illustrated cover was drawn by Robert H. Barlow. In a prefatory note HPL states: "This book consists of ideas, images, & quotations, hastily jotted down for possible future use in weird fiction. Very few are actually developed plots - for the most part they are merely suggestions or random impressions designed to set the memory or imagination cranking. Their sources are various - dreams, things read, casual incidents, idle conceptions, & so on." This book is inscribed: "Presented to R. H. Barlow, Esq. on May 7, 1934 - in exchange for an admirably neat typed copy from his skilled hand." The typed version is Accession A55361 [170]. First published in Barlow's edition of The Notes & Commonplace Book employed by the late H. P. Lovecraft (Lakeport, CA: The Futile Press, 1938).
creator: Lovecraft, H.P.
subject: fantasy fiction; horror tales; American; twentieth century; drawings; manuscript; personal notes; quotations

title: H.P. Lovecraft's Commonplace Book
description: Lovecraft asked Barlow to type up his notes from his original Commonplace Book (Accession A322500 [3]). This is Barlow's typed version of the manuscript Commonplace Book. This small loose-leaf notebook contains manuscript additions by HPL at the end. First published in Barlow's edition of The Notes & Commonplace Book employed by the late H. P. Lovecraft (Lakeport, CA: The Futile Press, 1938).
creator: Lovecraft, H.P.
subject: fantasy fiction; horror tales; American; twentieth century; manuscript; personal notes; quotations

title: North African commonplace book in Arabic
description:
Manuscript codex; Title devised by cataloger. Physical description: Varying lines per page, written in maghribī script on heavy laid European paper with large crown watermark (e. g. leaf 109). Leaves 2b, 38b, 58a, 73a blank. Some staining; a few leaves have repairs to the inner margin, resulting in slight loss of text. Mild insect damage. Disbound.

A commonplace book consisting primarily of poetry quotations with their accompanying explanatory text in prose, particularly from Abū al-Faraj's Kitāb al-aghānī (e. g. leaves 6a, 10a, 16a, etc. ). On leaf 116a is a list of the years between successive prophetic revelations; on leaves 118a, 119a are schematic diagrams of buildings.

Loose in red leather wrapper with flap; blind-stamped central mandorla, blind-tooled fillets, gold-stamped quatrefoils in corners, gold tooled border around mandorla, gold-tooled chain extending vertically from top and bottom of mandorla to fillets. Spine split apart.

Forms part of the Harry L. and Mary K. Dalton Collection (David M. Rubenstein Rare Book & Manuscript Library, Duke University).
subject: eighteenth century; poetry; African; manuscript; Maghribi; architecture; illustration

title: Ann Jordan album; or selections from many authors; interspersed with drawings and poetry
description: An illustrated commonplace album with transcribed verses, watercolors, and drawings; the album was a gift to Ann Jordan from her brother W. P. Jordan in 1828. The album contains 27 watercolor illustrations including flowers and butterflies (the latter usually cut and pasted to various leaves), and nine pencil sketches. Also included are poems by Thomas H. Bayly, Mary Ann Browne, Lady Byron, Lord Byron, Benjamin Franklin, Oliver Goldsmith, Peter Pindar (whose real name was John Wolcot), Ann Radcliffe, and George R. Sims. The album contains a number of blank pages and is bound in contemporary red half morocco; the corners and spine are worn and damaged.
creator: Jordan, Ann
subject: nineteenth century; poetry; illustration; drawings; botanical illustration; English; human ecology; lepidopterists; ornithology; women; botany

title: Commonplace book of Matthew Day
description: Commonplace book of Latin aphorisms (Part I: p. 1-9). Poetical miscellany (Part II: p. 1-107). Among the poets mentioned are Denham, Thomas Dekker, Walter Raleigh, Randolph, Herrick, King, Thomas May and Shakespeare to whom a poem on the King is attributed (p. 2, Part II). Probably collected by Matthew Day who was mayor of Windsor. Also, a work on the five senses.
creator: Day, Matthew
subject: seventeenth century; aphorisms; poetry; manuscript
